register: Private aviation operators — plain English, no insider jargon. NEVER use: 'Part 135', 'ARGUS', 'Wyvern', 'fractional ownership', 'jet card', 'empty-leg', 'flight department KPI', 'HNW'. Replace with: 'private jet charter company', 'people who fly private', 'private flight shopper', 'inquiry form', 'quote request', 'high-net-worth'. Tone: discreet, surgical, neutral, composed. No hype, no slang, no emoji, no high-pressure. Jeremy Miner NEPQ + Sandler Negative Reverse + PAS structure. Tony is the strategist — all calls book on his calendar.

A.name: Pattern Interrupt + Soft Disarm
A.desc: NEPQ + PAS. Permission-based open, 8-second window problem, two leaks revealed with a third withheld for the meeting. Best for never-engaged operators with verified phone.

beats.title: Disarm + Permission
beats.line: Appreciate you picking up — I know this is out of the blue. You're with {company}, correct? Every quarter we do a quiet review of private aviation websites — looking at what a quote shopper actually sees before they ever fill out the form. Got 60 seconds, or did I catch you bad?
beats.followup: Fair enough — I'll be quick. 60 seconds, and you decide if it's worth more.
beats.note: Miner low-status open. Pause after 'quote shopper actually sees.' Don't fill silence. Composure is the brand.

beats.title: The 8-Second Window
beats.line: Quick context — when someone with the means to fly private lands on a charter site, there's about an 8-second window where they decide whether you're serious or not quite there yet. Most operators lose them inside that window. Not price, not the aircraft — three structural things on the page. When's the last time you actually pulled up your own site on a phone and tried to request a quote end-to-end?
beats.followup: Most operators say that — and the page usually looks fine. The leak is rarely visual. It's structural.
beats.note: PAS — Problem. The 8-second window is universally true in luxury commerce. NEPQ question forces self-reflection without confrontation.

beats.title: Two Leaks Revealed, Third Withheld
beats.line: On {company} specifically, two things jumped out. First — {leak_1}. Second — {leak_2}. Neither is fatal alone, but on flights booking at $25,000 to $80,000, even a small drop in inquiry-to-booking is real revenue walking off the page every week. There's a third leak that costs more than both — I'd rather walk you through it on a 15-minute screen share than rush it on the phone.
beats.followup: The third one's the easiest to fix and the most expensive to ignore. Worth 15 minutes.
beats.note: DO NOT reveal leak 3 on the phone. The third leak IS the meeting. If they push: 'I'd rather earn the 15 minutes than spoil it. Thursday or Friday?'

beats.title: Why Us + Discretion
beats.line: Quick on us — we work only with private aviation. Charter companies, shared-ownership, on-demand. No leisure, no commercial. And we're discreet — no public case studies, no name-drops, no posts about who we work with. Same discretion you'd want for your own clients.
responses: Who else have you worked with?
beats.followup: References after the review call, if it's the right fit on both sides.
beats.note: Plain English only. NEVER name-drop specific operators — discretion IS the product. If pressed for credentials, route to the review call.

beats.title: Free $4,500 Written Review
beats.line: What we typically do — whether we end up working together or not — is a written Website Conversion Review. Normally $4,500, yours to keep. Would it be unreasonable to have {strategist} spend 15 minutes walking you through the 3–5 structural fixes losing you quote requests, what each one is costing per week, and what your current traffic could actually be producing?
beats.followup: No catch — {strategist} is personally running a small number this quarter. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Always say {strategist} (Tony) as the strategist. The $4,500 anchor is the value frame — keep the document concept tangible.

beats.title: Lock the Time
beats.line: Best email so I can send the calendar invite — and would Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern work better with {strategist}? He blocks it himself, so whichever you pick is locked.
beats.followup: Perfect — let me read the email back to make sure I have it right.
beats.note: Two specific slots, not 'whenever works.' Discreet clients respect bounded calendars. Always read email back verbatim — wrong email is the #1 no-show cause.

B.name: Curiosity Hook + Sandler Negative Reverse
B.desc: NEPQ Belief-Building + Sandler. Lowers resistance by telling the prospect you're easy to say no to. Best for re-engagement, referrals, or operators who ghosted on a prior touch.

beats.title: Curiosity Hook
beats.line: Hi — {caller} with ConversionJet. I work with private jet charter operators on something that quietly costs most of them about 30% of the quote requests they're paying to generate. 60 seconds to tell you what it is? If it doesn't apply to {company}, I'll let you go.
beats.followup: Not selling anything on this call — just telling you what I see on your site. You decide if it's worth more.
beats.note: If they ask 'what is it?' — you've won the open. Slow down. Composure signals you're not the usual agency cold-caller.

beats.title: Build the Belief
beats.line: When someone with the means to fly private lands on a charter site, there's an 8-second window where they decide whether you're serious or not. Most operators lose them around the 6-second mark. Not price, not the airplanes — three structural things on the page. When's the last time you pulled up your own site on a phone and walked it like a buyer would?
beats.followup: Most operators say that — and the page looks fine. The leak is rarely visual. It's structural.
beats.note: Belief-building. The NEPQ self-test question gets them to mentally simulate their own buyer journey.

beats.title: Two Leaks Revealed, Third Withheld
beats.line: I pulled up {company} before this call. {leak_1} is one. {leak_2} is the second. On flights booking at $25,000 to $80,000, even a small drop in form-fill rate is real money quietly leaving every week. The third leak costs more than both — and it's the easiest to fix — but I'd rather show it to you on a screen share than rush it now.
beats.followup: Thursday or Friday — worth 15 minutes. Worst case, you've still got two free fixes from this call.
beats.note: Hold the third leak. If they push: 'I'd rather earn the time than spoil it. Thursday or Friday?'

beats.title: Lower the Stakes (Sandler Negative Reverse)
beats.line: Look — this isn't a sales call. {strategist} walks you through what we'd change and in what order. Then you either do it in-house, hand it to your existing team, or — if it's the right fit on both sides — we do it for you. Most operators take the document and run with it. That's fine. We'd rather you do that than feel pushed.
beats.followup: No catch — {strategist} is personally running a small number this quarter. And we don't publish names. Truly anonymous.
beats.note: Sandler Negative Reverse: telling them you're EASY to say no to drops resistance. Discreet clients hate being sold to.

beats.title: Free $4,500 Written Review
beats.line: What we'd do — whether we work together or not — is a written Website Conversion Review, normally $4,500, yours to keep. {strategist} spends 15 minutes walking you through the structural fixes, what each one's costing per week, and the quote requests your current traffic could be producing. Whether you act on it or shelve it is entirely your call.
responses: What do you need from me?
beats.followup: Almost nothing on your end — best email, and {strategist} does the rest live on the call.
beats.note: Frame the value, not the pitch. The audit is the door, not the close.

beats.title: Close on Time
beats.line: Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern — which works better? {strategist} blocks it himself, so whichever you pick is locked. Best email for the invite?
beats.followup: Perfect — let me read the email back so I have it right.
beats.note: Two specific slots. Read email back verbatim.

C.name: Paid-Search Leak + The Math
C.desc: PAS + Specificity Cluster + Loss-Frame. Math-led — anchors on what they're paying per click and what the lift is worth. Best for operators visibly running paid search (verify on Google before dialing).

beats.title: Paid-Search Anchor
beats.line: Quick reason for the call — I see {company} is running paid search on {primary_kw}. Clicks in your category are running ${cpc_low} to ${cpc_high} each right now. Got 60 seconds?
beats.followup: Not about the ads — the ads are doing their job. The leak is on what happens after the click.
beats.note: PRE-CALL: verify they actually run paid search by Googling their primary keyword from {market}. If you don't see them on the SERP, switch to Variant A or B.

beats.title: The Math, Fast
beats.line: Fast math — if your click costs ${cpc_high} and roughly 1.5 out of every 100 clicks fills out a quote form, you're paying low thousands per real inquiry. Move that page from 1.5% to 3% — well inside normal range for a properly built private aviation site — and you cut your cost-per-quote in half. Same spend, double the quote requests.
beats.followup: Higher than 3% or higher than 1.5%? Big difference — most operators we review land between 0.8% and 2.2%.
beats.note: Don't argue numbers. Anchor on the LIFT, not the current state. If they claim 5%+, ask: 'Measured at quote-submit, or at booked-flight?'

beats.title: Where the Spend Is Leaking
beats.line: I pulled up where your ad spend is landing. Two things jumped out — {leak_1}, and {leak_2}. Both are afternoon fixes for a developer. The third leak is the most expensive of the three — and that one I'd rather walk you through on a screen share with your page open, because it's specific to how {company} is set up.
beats.followup: The review itself is free. The fix depends on what we find — some operators do it in-house once they see the diagnosis.
beats.note: 'Afternoon fix' makes the win feel close. Third-leak withhold creates the meeting pull. Loss-Frame: the spend is already leaking, the only question is how long.

beats.title: Why Us + Discretion
beats.line: Quick on us — we work only with private aviation. Charter, shared-ownership, on-demand. No leisure, no commercial. We're discreet — names stay between us and the principal. Same discretion you'd expect for your own clients.
beats.followup: References after the review call, if it's the right fit on both sides.
beats.note: Plain English only. NEVER name-drop on a cold call. Discretion IS the differentiator.

beats.title: Free $4,500 Written Review
beats.line: Whether we work together or not — we'll run a written Website Conversion Review. Normally $4,500, yours to keep. Would it be unreasonable to have {strategist} spend 15 minutes walking you through exactly where the ad spend is leaking, what each click is actually worth, and the 3–5 fixes that produce more quote requests from the traffic you're already paying for?
beats.followup: No catch — {strategist} is personally running a small number this quarter. That's the trade.
beats.note: Always {strategist} as the strategist. Free review is the door — never the close.

beats.title: Lock the Time
beats.line: Best email so I can send the calendar invite — and would Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern work better? {strategist} will have the review pulled up live, screen share, the full walkthrough.
beats.followup: Perfect — let me read the email back to make sure I have it right. Anyone else from your side who should be on the call — usually whoever owns the website?
beats.note: Read email back verbatim. Confirm second attendee — pulling in the web owner up front raises show-up rate and shortens the cycle.
